title: Generalised Body Heaviness / Extreme Fatigue
summary: Overwhelming physical fatigue and body heaviness that is disproportionate to activity. In Africa, this symptom pattern commonly indicates malaria, anaemia, typhoid, or severe dehydration — all of which are treatable.
steps: Take this symptom seriously in an African context. Extreme fatigue with body heaviness is a key early symptom of malaria, anaemia, and typhoid — do not dismiss it as just tiredness.

Check for accompanying symptoms: fever, chills, headache (malaria pattern). Pallor of inner eyelids and gums, shortness of breath with minimal activity (anaemia pattern). Sustained fever with abdominal discomfort (typhoid pattern). Recent illness, vomiting, diarrhoea (dehydration pattern).
Check hydration status: is the urine dark yellow? Is the mouth dry? Is there headache? Dehydration alone causes significant body fatigue — push fluids first.
Check the colour of the inside of the lower eyelid. Pull it down gently. Normal pink indicates normal haemoglobin. Pale white or very pale pink indicates possible anaemia.
Rest completely. Do not push through extreme fatigue in an acute illness — it worsens the condition.
Push fluids — water and oral rehydration solution. Aim for urine that is pale yellow.
Eat iron-rich food if anaemia is suspected — liver, red meat, beans, dark leafy vegetables.
If malaria symptoms are present — act on the malaria guidance immediately. This is the most urgent possibility.
Monitor the temperature twice daily. Document all symptoms and their timeline for the doctor.
warnings: Do not assume extreme fatigue is normal in a sick person — it can be the primary symptom of a serious treatable illness.
Fatigue with pallor in a pregnant woman is a medical emergency — severe anaemia in pregnancy is dangerous.
Fatigue following a long illness, surgery, or significant blood loss needs medical assessment for anaemia.
Persistent unexplained fatigue lasting more than 2 weeks without fever or other obvious cause needs investigation.
redFlags: Extreme fatigue with fever and chills — test for malaria immediately.
Fatigue with pallor, breathlessness at rest, and rapid heartbeat — severe anaemia.
Fatigue with jaundice — yellow skin or eyes.
Fatigue with weight loss and night sweats — possible tuberculosis.
Fatigue with persistent fever above 38°C for more than 3 days.
Person cannot sit up or perform basic self-care.

helpful: Malaria rapid diagnostic test — test first before anything else in endemic areas
Oral rehydration salts if dehydration is contributing
Ferrous sulphate tablets if anaemia is confirmed or suspected
avoid: Energy drinks and stimulants — they mask symptoms without treating the cause
Pushing through extreme fatigue in an acute illness
askFor: Malaria RDT test at a pharmacy
Full blood count at a clinic to check for anaemia
Ferrous sulphate for iron deficiency anaemia

whenToGetHelp: See a doctor or health worker for extreme fatigue lasting more than 3 days, fatigue with fever, fatigue with pallor especially in pregnancy, or fatigue that prevents normal daily activity. In malaria-endemic areas, always rule out malaria first.
